Browse by flow

Switch interview mode

4 flows
High-level design interview prep HLD
Architecture, distributed systems trade-offs, and end-to-end system walkthroughs for classic system design rounds.
Low-level design interview prep LLD
Object modeling, extensible APIs, machine-coding structure, and follow-up handling for class-design rounds.
Data structures and algorithms interview prep DSA
Curated coding-round practice across general problem sets and company-specific question banks for array, tree, graph, DP, and mock-round prep.
AI Engineer learning path AI/ML
End-to-end AI/ML engineering from foundations through LLMs, RAG, agents, MLOps, and responsible AI for building production intelligent systems.

Curriculum → Low-level design interview prep

LLD interview flow

Low-level design interview prep

Use this flow when the interview focuses on classes, interfaces, workflows, patterns, testing seams, and how code structure adapts to changing requirements.

Frame the scope first, sketch the smallest coherent object model, then explain how your contracts absorb follow-up requirements without collapsing into a god class.

Flow progress 0/12

Lessons complete across 4 modules

Start here LLD foundations and prompt framing

Learn how to turn a machine-coding or object-design prompt into bounded requirements, clean responsibilities, and explicit object contracts.

Best for Best for SDE-1 to senior engineer rounds that test object-oriented design, machine coding, and code-level architecture judgment.

Work module by module and code small examples alongside the lessons so the design vocabulary turns into implementation muscle memory.

Target outcome You should be able to produce a clean class design quickly, justify pattern choices, and extend the design under interviewer follow-ups without rewriting everything.

Move through the modules in order so each later interview answer inherits a stable mental model.

4 modules 12 lessons LLD roadmap Open next module Browse modules

Core focus

What this flow trains

  1. Prompt framing, invariants, and responsibility assignment Keep tying the design back to the constraint or follow-up that makes this area matter in interviews.
  2. Object modeling, workflow orchestration, and change-friendly abstractions Keep tying the design back to the constraint or follow-up that makes this area matter in interviews.
  3. Machine-coding execution, testing seams, concurrency, and scale follow-ups Keep tying the design back to the constraint or follow-up that makes this area matter in interviews.
LLD specific Reusable module sequence

How to use it

Study rhythm

  • Open the next incomplete module and finish one lesson end to end.
  • Explain the main trade-off or object-model decision out loud before moving on.
  • Use the lesson practice lab to save a draft answer when the prompt calls for it.

Signals to build

What interviewers usually reward

  • Clear scoping and explicit assumptions before detailed design.
  • Clean boundaries between responsibilities, data, and side effects.
  • Trade-off awareness when requirements or follow-up constraints change.

Navigation

Keep the path obvious

  • Use the module runway below when you want the full sequence.
  • Use the global sidebar to jump between HLD and LLD at any time.
  • Track completion locally so interrupted practice sessions resume cleanly.

Module runway

Follow the LLD sequence from first module to capstone

Each module builds one interview muscle before the next one adds more complexity.

0/12 complete

Module

LLD foundations and prompt framing

Learn how to turn a machine-coding or object-design prompt into bounded requirements, clean responsibilities, and explicit object contracts.

  • Clarify scope, actors, and invariants before sketching classes
  • Model responsibilities so each type owns one coherent reason to change
  • Choose interfaces and APIs that survive follow-up requirements
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Object modeling and domain relationships

Practice identifying entities, value objects, aggregates, and collaborations so your designs stay cohesive as requirements grow.

  • Separate identity-bearing entities from immutable values
  • Choose composition, association, or inheritance based on behavior
  • Keep state and workflows aligned with domain boundaries
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Extensibility and common LLD patterns

Use patterns as decision tools, not cargo cults, so your design stays open to change without drowning in abstraction.

  • Apply core patterns only when they solve a real axis of change
  • Use dependency inversion to isolate volatile collaborators
  • Keep pattern-heavy answers readable under interview time pressure
3 lessons 0/3 complete

Module

Machine coding, testing, and follow-up handling

Rehearse how to present class design, write extensible code under time pressure, and adapt cleanly when the interviewer adds new requirements.

  • Structure machine-coding answers so core classes appear quickly
  • Design for testability, concurrency, and follow-up changes
  • Narrate trade-offs while keeping the implementation incremental
3 lessons 0/3 complete
